fact: The Iroquois Theatre advertised itself as 'absolutely fireproof'
source: Timetable of World Legal History: 1903 — widely documented; detailed in 'Chicago Death Trap: The Iroquois Theatre Fire of 1903' by Nat Brandt (2003)

fact: The theater had no sprinklers, no fire alarms, and exit doors that only opened inward
source: Chicago Death Trap: The Iroquois Theatre Fire of 1903, Nat Brandt (2003); also corroborated by the Chicago History Museum's records on building code violations

fact: About two thousand people, mostly women and children, attended a holiday matinee when a spotlight ignited a muslin curtain backstage
source: Chicago Death Trap: The Iroquois Theatre Fire of 1903, Nat Brandt (2003); contemporary accounts in the Chicago Tribune, December 31, 1903

fact: The asbestos safety curtain snagged halfway down on a wire and failed to seal the stage from the audience
source: Chicago Death Trap, Nat Brandt (2003); also detailed in 'The Great Chicago Theater Disaster' by Marshall Everett (1904)

fact: A stagehand opened the rear loading door, which fed a blast of air that sent flames into the auditorium
source: Chicago Death Trap, Nat Brandt (2003); widely documented in fire-science literature as a backdraft event

fact: 602 people died and the theater had been open for only five weeks (opened November 23, 1903; fire on December 30, 1903)
source: National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) records; Chicago Death Trap, Nat Brandt (2003)
